analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Aljamain Sterling -- Style & Profile

- Sterling built his career on elite grappling and suffocating clinch work, but his last three fights tell a different story — he averaged barely 1.09 significant strikes per minute and attempted zero submissions across all three. He's fighting like a counter-mover who occasionally shoots, not the grappler who choked out Cory Sandhagen in 90 seconds.

- His striking accuracy has held up reasonably well (career 52%, hit 61% against Ortega in his most recent win), but the volume is anemic for a featherweight main event. At 1.08 SLpM in his last outing, he was barely active — that works against shot fighters but not against someone with Zalal's submission threat.

- The takedown numbers are the biggest red flag. Sterling's career average is 2.45 per 15 minutes, but his takedown accuracy sits at just 30% — and across his last three fights he's combined for almost no submission attempts. Against Zalal, whose own takedown defense is at 59%, Sterling can get to the ground, but converting those positions into finishes looks increasingly unlikely.

- He lost his most recent meaningful bout to Movsar Evloev on the scorecards after being almost entirely shut out (0.2 SLpM, 27% accuracy), which suggests the elite-level defensive grapplers and active wrestlers in this division have found ways to neutralize him. Zalal isn't that profile, but the passivity in Sterling's recent outputs is a real concern.

- At 36, Sterling is at the age cliff for men in the lighter weight classes. He beat Ortega — a shot, aging fighter himself — and he beat Kattar, who is primarily a striker with defensive grappling limitations. Neither result tells us Sterling is back to peak form.

- He can still win close fights. His defensive numbers remain solid (59% strike defense, only 2.21 SLpM absorbed career-wide), and five-round main events have historically suited his grinding, attrition-based style. But the ceiling on his offensive output looks lower than it did two years ago.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Youssef Zalal -- Style & Profile

- Zalal has quietly become one of the most credible submission threats in the featherweight division. Four of his last six wins have come by submission, including two first-round finishes and a stunning rear-naked choke of Josh Emmett — a durable, battle-tested former title contender — in under two minutes.

- He switches stances and works well off the back foot, keeping a 66% career strike defense while absorbing only 1.78 significant strikes per minute. He doesn't look to brawl; he uses movement to create takedown entries and scrambles, which is exactly the environment where his submission game thrives.

- The submission threat isn't just from the bottom — against Emmett, he initiated with a takedown, ended up in a scramble, and locked up the finish. He's averaging 1.4 submission attempts per 15 minutes over his career, which is meaningful volume for a 145-pounder, and those attempts are increasingly landing against quality opponents.

- His striking output is modest (3.03 SLpM career, averaging 1.73 in his last three fights outside the Emmett grapple-fest), but his accuracy is solid at 50-56% in recent fights. He's not going to out-box Sterling, and he probably doesn't need to — he just needs to keep the striking close enough to create takedown moments.

- The Calvin Kattar decision win is genuinely impressive context. Kattar is a high-volume, accurate striker who tends to make opponents pay for sloppy entries. Zalal kept that fight competitive on the feet and controlled enough of the grappling to win the cards. That's a relevant résumé marker against a wrestler-striker hybrid like Sterling.

- His takedown accuracy (31% career) is middling, but when he hits his shots, he finishes sequences — the Emmett fight showed 9.18 takedowns per 15 minutes in the round it happened, meaning once he got his rhythm, he didn't stop.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This fight comes down to a simple but important question: which version of each fighter shows up? Sterling is the more accomplished grappler on paper, but Zalal is the more active and dangerous one in practice right now. Sterling's last three fights averaged barely one significant strike per minute and zero submission attempts — he's become a defensive grinder rather than the aggressive clinch-and-sub threat who built his legacy. Zalal, meanwhile, is on a five-fight win streak with four finishes, including a submission of Josh Emmett that should not be treated as a fluke. The momentum disparity here is stark.

The critical variable is who ends up on top. Sterling wants to control the clinch, use cage pressure, and grind through the round with his cage work and top control. Zalal wants scrambles — transitions, back-and-forth grappling exchanges, and precisely the kind of loose, chaotic moments where he laces up chokes. Sterling's takedown defense is only 44%, which is a real problem. If Zalal can match Sterling's takedown output or simply survive Sterling's shots long enough to create scrambles, his submission game is live every time they hit the mat. Conversely, if Sterling gets top control consistently and grinds without taking risks, he can win rounds on the judges' cards — it just requires activity he hasn't shown lately.

The age gap matters in a five-round main event. Sterling is seven years older and just had his first fight in eight months — his 245-day layoff, combined with the visible decline in his offensive production, creates real questions about where his cardio lands in rounds four and five. Zalal is 29, fresh off a peak-performance submission win, and has shown no late-round dropoff in his recent run. The intangibles lean Zalal: younger, hotter, more dangerous in extended grappling exchanges. Sterling needs to be disciplined, active enough to steal rounds, and good enough on the feet to avoid the scrambles where Zalal is at his most dangerous. That's a lot to ask of a 36-year-old who posted 0.2 SLpM in his last loss.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Youssef Zalal wins by submission, Rounds 1–3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Zalal's submission threat is real, proven, and active — he's finished four of his last six opponents this way, including the kind of scramble-finish that Sterling's 44% takedown defense makes him vulnerable to. Sterling's reluctance to engage offensively in recent fights means the grappling will be contested rather than dominated, and every time it hits the mat in a scramble, Zalal is the more dangerous finisher. The combination of Zalal's submission volume and Sterling's declining offensive output on the ground makes this the most credible path.

**#2 -- Aljamain Sterling wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Sterling fights the way he beat Ortega — patient, defensive, just active enough to take rounds — he can grind out a five-round decision. His strike defense remains solid and he can use cage pressure to control positioning without taking the scramble risks that expose him to submissions. This requires Sterling to be more offensively present than he was against Evloev, but it's structurally possible for a fighter of his experience and defensive IQ.

**#3 -- Youssef Zalal wins by decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Sterling survives the submission danger and fights conservatively enough to avoid getting finished, Zalal still has a credible path on the scorecards. He beat Kattar — a more active and accurate striker than Sterling has been recently — and his movement and counter-striking are sufficient to win rounds against a passive Sterling. In a long fight where neither man finds a finish, Zalal's youth and current momentum give him the edge on the cards.

**#4 -- Aljamain Sterling wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Sterling has the pedigree — he's a former two-time champion and legitimately elite grappler — but his last three fights show zero submission attempts. His finishing instinct on the ground appears to have faded, even when he's in dominant positions. It's not impossible that he finds a choke or arm lock against Zalal, but nothing in his recent outputs suggests he's hunting finishes the way he used to.
analysis_sections.betting:
**PLAY — Youssef Zalal**
Confidence: 62%

**The pick:** Zalal at +135 (current market). The line has moved significantly in Sterling's favor from the open — Sterling opened at -155 and has drifted out to -110, while Zalal moved from +115 to +135. This is not reverse line movement; money has come in on both sides, but the line has lengthened on Zalal, suggesting some sharp interest on Sterling tightened it slightly before widening back. The bottom line is that the current number on Zalal represents real value for an underdog who is the hotter, more dangerous, more active fighter.

**Why the market is mispriced:** At +135, Zalal is being offered as a ~43% implied probability against a 36-year-old former champion who has posted one genuinely impressive recent win (over a very shot Brian Ortega) and one significant loss (to Evloev via decision shutout). Sterling's offensive output has cratered — his last three fights averaged 0.76 SLpM. In a five-round main event against a submission specialist with a 44% takedown defense, the incumbent's age, layoff, and declining production justify treating this closer to a coin flip or a slight Zalal edge. The market is still weighting Sterling's name and legacy. The data supports Zalal.

**Line:** Zalal +135
**Implied probability:** 42.6%
**Assessed probability:** 58%
**Stake:** 1.5 units

**Target line:** The bet still has value up to approximately +108. If you're shopping books and Zalal is available at +120 or better, the play stands. Below +108, pass.

**Both fighters' data:** Sterling has more than 10 UFC fights with full per-fight splits and recent activity — his data is solid but his most recent relevant stats show a concerning trend. Zalal has a growing UFC sample with five fights in the data window, including a fight as recent as October 2025. Both carry enough data to support a play, though note that intangibles from Tapology were unavailable for both fighters — any late-camp changes or weight-cut issues are unaccounted for.

**Market read:** Sterling opened at -155, current -110 — a significant lengthening of around 45 cents on the favorite. Zalal opened +115, now +135. The movement pattern suggests some early Sterling money tightened it, then action pushed back. No confirmed reverse line movement, but the line has clearly moved toward a closer fight than the books initially expected. This is broadly consistent with the stat-based read — the market has been adjusting toward Zalal's value, just not far enough.

**Hedge verdict — NO ARB:** Best available line is Sterling approximately -110 and Zalal +135. Combined implied probability: (-110 → 52.4%) + (+135 → 42.6%) = 95.0%. That's inside the arb threshold. At 95% combined implied, there is technically a **marginal arb** opportunity.

- Total stake: $100
- Zalal +135 (decimal 2.35): stake = 100 × (2.10 / (2.35 + 2.10)) = $47.19 → payout $110.90
- Sterling -110 (decimal 1.91): stake = 100 × (2.35 / (2.35 + 2.10)) = $52.81 → payout $100.87

The guaranteed profit is slim (roughly $0.87 on $100, under 1% ROI), making this a marginal arb in name only — transaction costs and line movement will eat it. **The recommendation is not to hedge; take the value pick on Zalal at +135 for 1.5 units and let it ride.** The edge is in the pick, not the structure.

**Props worth considering:** Given both fighters' recent outputs — Zalal averaging 1.73 SLpM in his last two non-grapple fights, Sterling at 0.64 across his last three — the total significant strikes will be low if the fight stays competitive on the feet. A "fight goes to decision" no-prop is worth examining if available at under -150. More interestingly, a Zalal submission finish prop (if offered at around +200 or better) has genuine value given four submission finishes in his last six fights. Sterling's 44% takedown defense and the five-round format both amplify that probability in the later rounds if neither man has found a finish early.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is a volume-control striker who wins by doing just enough — she lands at 3.89 significant strikes per minute over her UFC career but absorbs only 2.12, the clearest signal that she's managing distance and dictating pace rather than brawling. Her 65% strike defense is among the better marks in the bantamweight division.
- She is 9-for-9 in decisions over her last 10 fights. This is not a coincidence or a finishing-rate anomaly — she has zero KOs and zero submissions in that stretch. Dumont wins by outworking opponents on the feet, staying clean, and accumulating rounds. She is not built to finish.
- Her last-3 strike output is actually trending downward from her career average — 1.13 SLpM against Vieira, 2.13 against Aldana, near-zero against de Randamie (where she leaned on takedowns). She is landing *less* than her already-modest career number, suggesting she plays conservatively against serious opponents.
- That de Randamie fight is worth flagging: she threw almost nothing on the feet and instead used her 1.56 TD average to control the action. Her secondary mode is a measured clinch and takedown game, not heavy leather. She is fundamentally a fighter who goes where she's most comfortable rather than forcing confrontations.
- At 35, she is at the age where decision-heavy fighters start to lose close rounds — the pace advantage that made her style work in her early-30s is under pressure. She has not been knocked out in her UFC run, but she's also never faced anyone with sustained finishing ability in the last three years who was genuinely elite.
- The 175-day layoff since her last fight is roughly a standard turnaround and doesn't raise a major ring-rust flag, but her pattern of declining output in big fights does suggest she may be past the peak of her particular game.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Joselyne Edwards -- Style & Profile

- Edwards has undergone a striking evolution that is almost jarring to read on paper. She put up 11.92 SLpM against Chelsea Chandler and 10.83 against Priscila Cachoeira — roughly triple the activity she showed in earlier UFC fights. Both fights ended in first-round KOs. This is not the same fighter the books priced six months ago.
- Her most recent fight against Nora Cornolle showed a completely different look — just 0.65 SLpM and a submission finish in Round 2. The ability to win both ways (violent KO pace *and* clean submission) in back-to-back fights signals real combat flexibility, even if the stats across those three fights look wildly inconsistent.
- Defensively, she is a real concern: 50% strike defense and 3.24 significant strikes absorbed per minute are both well below Dumont's defensive benchmarks. She is willing to trade, and she sometimes eats shots to land her own. Against a tidy counter-puncher like Dumont, that tendency is relevant.
- Her takedown defense sits at 61%, which is below average for the division, and her takedown accuracy is only 40%. She is not primarily a grappler — but she showed 5.96 TDs per 15 minutes against Chandler, suggesting she *can* be physical when she wants the ground. The submission against Cornolle reinforces this.
- The 63-day turnaround from her last fight (February 2026) is a non-issue from a rest standpoint, and she's 30 years old — no age-curve concern here. She is physically fresh and coming off the best three-fight stretch of her UFC career.
- The three-inch reach advantage she holds over Dumont is meaningful in a striking fight — Edwards can establish the jab and range control from the outside, which matters against a fighter who relies on being the one setting distance.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this fight is between Dumont's disciplined, low-risk game and Edwards's recent explosion of finishing violence. Dumont has spent her entire UFC run controlling distance, accumulating small striking advantages, and never putting herself in danger — she absorbs just 2.12 significant strikes per minute against a field that includes legitimate ranked opponents. Edwards, on the other hand, has produced two of the most one-sided performances on the bantamweight undercard in the last calendar year. The question is whether Dumont's system — which has never really been *tested* by someone with genuine finishing power — holds up against a fighter who just knocked out Chelsea Chandler in two and a half minutes.

Dumont's best path is exactly what she always does: establish distance, keep the fight clean in the middle of the cage, and outpoint Edwards over three rounds by landing the cleaner shots while moving away from the power. Her 65% strike defense is real, and she is disciplined enough not to chase. The problem is Edwards's reach. At 70 inches, Edwards has three inches on Dumont, and a fighter with recent finishing power who can maintain range is a genuinely dangerous profile for someone trying to be the smaller counter-striker. Dumont will need to be excellent at closing distance without eating punches, and her last-3 stats suggest she's trending toward *less* offensive output against difficult opponents, not more.

Edwards's path is straightforward: stay active, use her reach to keep Dumont from getting comfortable at range, and look for the finish she's been finding consistently. Her 61% takedown defense is the one real vulnerability — if Dumont decides to use her takedown game (as she did against de Randamie), she may be able to drag Edwards into the kind of grinding, uneventful rounds that the judges have been rewarding Dumont for years. But Dumont has largely shelved the wrestling lately, and against a fighter with this kind of recent momentum, the smart money is on Edwards setting the terms.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Norma Dumont wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont has gone to the scorecards in 9 of her last 10 fights, and her disciplined defensive style is specifically designed to survive fighters with more finishing intent than technical ability. If she can neutralize Edwards's reach advantage with her own footwork and make this a 25-minute patience contest, the judges who've been voting for her consistently may do so again. The issue is that Edwards's recent opponents haven't been anywhere near as defensively sound.

**#2 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Edwards has finished two opponents in a combined five minutes over her last two striking performances, and those finishes came against a fighter (Chandler) who Dumont also beat by decision — meaning Edwards just did in 2.5 minutes what Dumont did in 15. With the reach advantage and her current striking form, a big early finish is a genuine scenario, particularly if Dumont tries to close the distance aggressively and walks into a straight shot.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Dumont successfully keeps the fight from becoming a brawl — moving laterally, staying off the fence, and making Edwards chase — Edwards may struggle to generate the volume needed to dominate the scorecards. But Edwards's overall activity level (4.61 career SLpM) is higher than Dumont's, and a pace advantage over a 30-year-old tends to show on the cards even in a close fight.

**#4 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards just submitted Cornolle with what appeared to be an opportunistic grappling finish, and her 0.6 submission attempts per 15 minutes shows she's not afraid to go there. Dumont's 68% takedown defense is solid but not elite, and if Edwards gets her to the mat in a clinch scramble rather than a traditional single-leg sequence, there's a path to a submission that the odds market is almost certainly not pricing.

**Recommendation: LEAN — Joselyne Edwards**
Confidence: 57%

The line tells a real story here. Edwards opened as a slight *favorite* at -195, and the market has shifted to Dumont at -225 while Edwards has moved out to +170. That is a significant drift toward the fighter whose last three fights consist of 9 straight decisions, and away from the fighter who just knocked out two opponents in the first round. The line movement is directionally consistent with sharp money, not reverse line movement, so the sharp side and the public side appear aligned on Dumont — but that doesn't mean the Edwards number isn't offering value.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Rafa Garcia -- Style & Profile

- Garcia is a grinding pressure fighter who wins by making fights ugly — he walks opponents down, grinds through attrition, and rarely lets anyone find a rhythm. His career numbers (4.31 strikes landed/min, 3.13 takedowns/15 min) reflect a fighter who mixes striking threats with wrestling to keep opponents uncomfortable throughout.

- His last three fights show a striking volume collapse that's hard to ignore: 1.77, 0.8, and 0.6 significant strikes per minute against his career average of 4.31. He is landing at a fraction of his historical rate, suggesting either a deliberate tactical shift toward wrestling control or a real decline in his ability to generate offense standing.

- The Grant Dawson KO loss in October 2024 was a second-round stoppage — an early finish — and it came from a fighter who is not known as a one-punch knockout artist. Garcia got hit cleanly and went down. He bounced back to finish Jared Gordon in Round 3 five months later, which is a meaningful response, but the chin question doesn't disappear after one good result.

- His takedown game is a genuine asset: 76% takedown defense is elite-level and his 44% takedown accuracy on offense means he can threaten both directions. When fights go to the mat, he's usually the more dangerous man — his one submission win and his grinding decision style both reflect someone who is comfortable eating time on the canvas.

- At 31, Garcia is well inside the age window where decline concerns are minimal. The 224-day layoff is a mild flag but not alarming given he was active through 2025.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Alexander Hernandez -- Style & Profile

- Hernandez is a power-output striker who looks for the finish and has found it three times in his last four completed fights. His two most recent wins — over Chase Hooper (R1 TKO) and Diego Ferreira (R2 TKO) — show a fighter who has rediscovered his finishing instincts after a rough patch in the middle of his career.

- The concerning part of his game is the striking accuracy: 40% career, and in his last three fights he's hitting 33%, 45%, and 32% — he's throwing with power but not always connecting cleanly. When he does land, opponents feel it. When he misses in volume, he opens himself up to counters, which explains why his absorbed rate (4.50/min career) is nearly as high as what he dishes out.

- His takedown numbers are weak across the board: 1.14 attempts per 15 minutes, 36% accuracy, 73% defense. He has not attempted a single takedown in two of his last three fights. He is essentially a pure striker at this point, and when the striking isn't working, he has no meaningful backup plan.

- At 33 in lightweight, he's approaching the window where the metrics on aging fighters start to bite — not alarmingly yet, but his strike defense (59% career) and the pattern of absorbing nearly as much as he throws means the margin for error shrinks as the chin ages.

- Hernandez has been knocked out before — Billy Quarantillo stopped him in Round 2 in 2022, and Renato Moicano submitted him the same year. He's won four straight since then, which is real progress, but he's not a fighter with an untested chin. The Quarantillo finish in particular was a clean stoppage against a legitimate power striker.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this fight is whether Hernandez can keep it standing long enough to land his power shots, or whether Garcia's wrestling pressure drags this into the dirty work where Hernandez is far less comfortable. Garcia's 76% takedown defense is elite, so he doesn't need to shoot constantly — the threat alone occupies Hernandez's attention. But Garcia's striking output has been minimal over his last three fights, which means he may not be able to win standing if the wrestling threat doesn't materialize. The fighter who controls where this fight takes place controls the outcome.

Hernandez's path to winning runs straight through early exchanges. He has real finishing power as evidenced by his recent run, and if he catches Garcia clean in rounds one or two before any wrestling-based fatigue sets in, he can end this fight. His accuracy issues are the problem — at 33-40% in recent outings, he's missing a lot, and Garcia's 61% strike defense is reasonable enough that Hernandez won't land freely. If Garcia can absorb the early pressure and push into wrestling-heavy exchanges by the second half of each round, Hernandez has shown no ability to neutralize takedowns consistently (73% defense, 36% accuracy on his own shots) and tends to fade in attrition-based fights.

The line has moved sharply toward Hernandez — opening as an even-money proposition and now sitting at -150 — which represents real market confidence in his current form. His four-fight win streak, including two recent stoppages, explains the bookmaker position. But Garcia is a live underdog here. His wrestling threat is a genuine differentiator in a fight where Hernandez can't grapple back effectively, and Garcia's chin question is mitigated somewhat by the fact that Hernandez, while capable of finishing, is doing it at 33-45% accuracy — not the profile of someone who will land the clean shot at will. The intangible delta between the two fighters is roughly neutral: both are 33 and 31 respectively, both are coming off a similar layoff, and neither has documented short-notice or weight-cut concerns in the available data.

**Flip conditions:** If Garcia's last-three striking collapse reflects injury or a genuine physical limitation rather than tactics, switch to Hernandez. If Hernandez's accuracy issues have been corrected in camp — something invisible in the data — the finishing threat becomes more credible. At -150, Hernandez is correctly priced only if his finishing rate against this level of opposition is repeatable. At +120, Garcia has value. At -108 or shorter, Garcia does not.

Hernandez at -150 is too expensive for a fighter whose accuracy has been in the 33-45% range and who can't grapple back when Garcia gets inside.

**Implied probability at +120:** 45.5%
**De-vigged market true probability:** approximately 42-43%
**Assessed probability:** approximately 48-50%
**Stake:** 0.5 units — this is a lean, not a play, and only at +110 or better
**Target line:** Garcia must be +105 or longer for this bet to retain value; do not take Garcia at -108 or shorter

**Market read:** Hernandez opened -100 (even) and has moved to -150 — the market has repriced him substantially on the back of his finish streak. There is no confirmed reverse line movement. The sharp money appears to be on Hernandez, which is a real check on the Garcia lean. If Garcia continues to shorten toward -110 or shorter, the bet is off entirely.

**Hedge verdict — NO ARB.** Garcia's best available is around -108 to +115 depending on the book; Hernandez sits at -150 to -140. Combined implied probability well exceeds 100% — no arbitrage opportunity exists. Take the value pick on the best available line on Garcia, do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** If Garcia is going to win, he's likely doing it by decision — a "fight goes to distance" YES prop could have value given both fighters' recent patterns (Garcia's last two fights both went to Round 3, Hernandez went to decision in two of his last four). Check the decision prop line; if it's priced around -120 or longer, it may have marginal value given how frequently these two fighters see the scorecards.

**Flip conditions:** If Garcia's last-three striking collapse reflects injury or a genuine physical limitation rather than tactics, switch to Hernandez. If Hernandez's accuracy issues have been corrected in camp — something invisible in the data — the finishing threat becomes more credible. At -150, Hernandez is correctly priced only if his finishing rate against this level of opposition is repeatable. At +120, Garcia has value. At -108 or shorter, Garcia does not.
<!--END-->

## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- Grant is 40 years old and fighting at a weight class where chins erode early and legs go first. His last three fights show someone surviving on experience and grit rather than physical tools — his striking volume has cratered, landing just 0.2 and 2.6 significant strikes per minute in back-to-back wins, both of which he ground out by decision.
- The most alarming signal is his most recent outing: a Round 1 submission loss to Charles Jourdain in just over three minutes. Jourdain is a striker, not a submission specialist — Grant got taken down and finished by a man whose primary weapons are his hands. That's a red flag about where Grant is physically right now.
- Historically, Grant's best weapon was his counter-punching power — three KO/TKO wins in his last ten, including late-round stoppages of Jonathan Martinez and Louis Smolka. But the last two years have shown him operating in a much more conservative mode, with zero takedown attempts in each of his last three fights.
- His strike defense sits at 55% career — barely above average for the division — and he's absorbing 3.82 significant strikes per minute. Against someone throwing at the volume Luna Martinetti brings, that number becomes a genuine problem.
- At 40 in a 135-pound division, Grant is operating well past the age cliff for lighter weight classes. Whatever physical advantages he once held — timing, speed, punch resistance — are diminished. He's been making fights ugly and winning close decisions, but that strategy has a ceiling against a younger, faster opponent.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- Luna Martinetti's career UFC stats are extreme: 11.0 significant strikes landed per minute at 65% accuracy. Even with the caveat that this is built on a single UFC fight, those numbers suggest an athlete with exceptional timing and output, not just a volume thrower — high accuracy at high volume is a rare combination.
- The flip side of those numbers is equally striking: 7.80 significant strikes absorbed per minute with just 48% strike defense. Luna Martinetti is not a defensive-minded fighter. He wins by overwhelming output, not by avoiding damage. That's a style that works until it doesn't.
- His one UFC fight — a unanimous decision over Mark Vologdin — produced notably more conservative in-fight numbers (2.87 SLpM, 57.3% accuracy) than his career averages suggest. Whether that reflects a calculated game plan, an opponent who neutralized his rhythm, or him taking time to calibrate to the UFC environment is genuinely unclear with a one-fight sample.
- His grappling numbers stand out for a 30-year-old striker with minimal ground offense: 3.0 takedowns per 15 minutes, 42% accuracy, and a listed 100% takedown defense. Again, one-fight sample — treat these as directional signals, not certainties.
- At 30 with a 17-1 record and only one UFC fight on his resume, Luna Martinetti is still an unknown quantity in terms of how he handles adversity. His pre-UFC career was built against different levels of competition, and his one UFC showing gave us a decision win but not a stress test.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important thing to understand about this fight is the age and trajectory gap. Grant is 40, moving backward physically, grinding out decisions with shrinking output. Luna Martinetti is 30, operating off career numbers that suggest a high-volume, high-accuracy striker who overwhelms opponents with pace. The stylistic question is whether Luna Martinetti's offense is real — and the answer from his career averages is yes, even discounting the one-fight UFC sample. Grant's strike defense at 55% career, trending toward the passive output we've seen in his last three fights, is not equipped to handle a fighter throwing at that rate with that accuracy.

Grant's only realistic path runs through two things: early pressure that disrupts Luna Martinetti's rhythm before he settles, or a grappling-heavy game plan that drags this to ugly scrambles. But Grant has attempted zero takedowns in his last three fights — whatever wrestling he used earlier in his career has effectively disappeared from his recent game. If Grant can't force a structural change in where the fight happens, he's standing in front of a younger, faster striker with better reach, better output, and no apparent quit. The submission loss to Jourdain is also still fresh — it showed Grant can be finished and that his physical tools are declining faster than his experience is compensating.

Luna Martinetti's concern is his own defense. Absorbing nearly 8 significant strikes per minute is a punishing rate, and Grant — when he finds his spots — still carries genuine knockout power. Three KO/TKO wins in his last ten fights, including against legitimate UFC fighters, means one right hand at the wrong moment can end this. Luna Martinetti also hasn't been stress-tested in the UFC yet. The net intangible picture still favors the younger fighter by a wide margin, but Grant's experience and residual power mean this isn't a complete blowout on paper — just a significant matchup disadvantage for a 40-year-old whose recent work says he's running on fumes.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Luna Martinetti's volume advantage is real, and Grant's recent output has been historically low. If Grant can't force a significant structural change early, Luna Martinetti outworks him across three rounds without needing a finish. Grant's durability and experience keep him in the fight long enough to reach the scorecards, but losing on volume is the most likely result here.

**#2 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by TKO/KO, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Grant's strike defense and the accumulation of damage at this stage of his career make a late stoppage plausible, especially if Luna Martinetti's output is closer to his career norms than his one UFC showing suggested. Grant tends to survive early but can deteriorate in the second half of fights when he's losing exchanges — and against a high-volume striker, the damage compounds.

**#3 -- Davey Grant wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Grant's blueprint for his last two wins was defensive, ugly, and tactically efficient — he can be disciplined and durable enough to frustrate a fighter who hasn't been extended in the UFC. If Luna Martinetti's defensive liabilities (-48% strike defense) allow Grant to steal rounds with his power shots while clinching and surviving, the judges could see it differently. Requires Luna Martinetti to underperform significantly from his career norms.

**#4 -- Davey Grant wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Grant still carries one-punch power, and Luna Martinetti absorbs significant volume. A single clean right hand in the opening rounds — before cardio and age fully kick in on Grant's side — remains a realistic if unlikely outcome. Grant's last clean KO win (Smolka) came in Round 3; his power is real but his execution has slowed.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- Jackson is built around his wrestling, not his striking. His career takedown rate of 3.0 per 15 minutes with 63% accuracy tells you what he wants to do — get it to the mat and grind. When he's performing at his best, he's dictating where the fight lives.

- His striking career averages look functional (3.09 SLpM, 52% accuracy), but his last two fights before the Blackshear KO reveal something concerning: against Marcos he threw at 0.27 SLpM with 33% accuracy, and against Figueiredo he managed 0.53 SLpM at 40%. Those aren't volume issues — that's a fighter who stops trusting his hands the moment the opponent is credible.

- The Blackshear KO at 0:18 of Round 1 inflated his last-3 striking average wildly. Strip that out and you're looking at a fighter who is generating almost nothing offensively on the feet against meaningful competition.

- His 61% strike defense and 1.40 absorbed per minute career average look excellent — but that absorption rate is almost certainly a byproduct of controlling range and keeping fights short with quick wrestling. Against Figueiredo, he was there all three rounds and that number looked very different.

- At 34, his reach advantage in this division is exceptional — 75 inches at bantamweight is rare. He knows how to use it to manage distance, jab behind it, and set up level changes. That's the stylistic backbone of his southpaw game.

- He has been knocked out before and has a 196-day layoff coming in. His chin has never been the issue, but the question is whether his striking confidence — already fragile against elite opponents — is further eroded after the Figueiredo loss.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- Barcelos is a high-volume striker by career averages — 4.92 SLpM — but his last three fights tell a completely different story. He averaged 1.07, 0.40, and 0.53 SLpM in those bouts, each a decision win. That gap between career identity and current output is enormous and suggests he has completely reinvented his approach, leaning into position and defensive work rather than offense.

- At 38, he has absorbed 4.40 significant strikes per minute across his UFC career — one of the higher absorption rates you'll see for a fighter still competing at this level. He has been knocked out (Umar Nurmagomedov, Round 1) and has gone the distance losing multiple decisions. The chin has taken meaningful mileage.

- His takedown defense is genuinely elite at 87%, which is the single most important number in this matchup. He cannot be easily taken down — and that is the one thing Jackson does best.

- Despite being labeled as a striker historically, his last three fights show him leaning heavily on takedowns and controlling output: 1.0 TD per 15 minutes across all three, with the Talbott fight showing 3.0 TD attempts per 15 minutes and a submission attempt. He is adapting to survive, not to finish.

- He has won three straight decisions against Ricky Simon, Cody Garbrandt, and Payton Talbott — none of them easy outs. That's real winning form at bantamweight depth, even if the performances were not exciting.

- The 8-inch reach disadvantage against Jackson is severe. At 67 inches, Barcelos will be fighting inside constantly, which plays directly into Jackson's jab-and-level-change game. Getting inside against a 75-inch southpaw with good wrestling is a difficult and punishing assignment.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this fight is that Jackson's primary weapon — the wrestling — runs directly into Barcelos's best defensive skill — an 87% takedown defense rate. Jackson shoots at 63% accuracy and takes down most opponents, but Barcelos is not most opponents on the mat side. If Jackson commits to wrestling and Barcelos is consistently stuffing those attempts, Jackson loses the main mechanism he relies on and is forced to fight a stand-up battle he has shown very little comfort in against quality competition. That is the matchup problem for the favorite.

On the feet, the 8-inch reach gap is decisive in Jackson's favor. Barcelos at 67 inches has to walk through jabs and level changes just to get into his range. Historically, Barcelos was a high-volume pressure fighter who could absorb punishment and keep coming — but the last three fights suggest that version of Barcelos no longer exists. He's fighting conservatively, winning on control and positioning, and generating almost nothing offensively. That's a fighter who is managing a declining toolkit, not improving one. At 38, with meaningful knockout damage in his history, the aggressive forward-pressure version of Barcelos that his career averages imply is not who Jackson is actually facing.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Jackson — he is four years younger, and the age curve matters in a sport where absorbing damage compounds over time. Barcelos at 38, carrying significant career mileage and a knockout loss on his record, is past the point where his body will respond the way it once did. Jackson's 196-day layoff is a mild concern but not disqualifying for a 3-round undercard fight. The flip side: if Barcelos's elite takedown defense nullifies the wrestling and Jackson reverts to the passive, low-output striking he showed against Marcos and Figueiredo, this becomes a very competitive fight that Barcelos — a veteran who knows how to steal rounds — is capable of winning.

**Flip conditions worth noting:** If Jackson's wrestling lands consistently in rounds two and three, this is a comfortable decision win for him. If Barcelos stuffs the first two or three shots and Jackson mentally abandons the takedown game, Barcelos's path to a decision widens considerably. The reach advantage keeps Jackson safe on the feet, but safe and winning are different things.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Montel Jackson wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Jackson's reach advantage, southpaw angles, and wrestling threat allow him to control range and dictate terms across three rounds without needing a finish. Even if his takedown success is limited by Barcelos's defense, the threat of the shot keeps Barcelos from fully committing offensively, and Jackson can win on distance management and activity without getting hurt. Barcelos's recent output has been too low to out-work him.

**#2 -- Raoni Barcelos wins by split or unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Barcelos has beaten three legitimate opponents in a row winning rounds through positioning and small-margins work. If he successfully defends two or three early takedown attempts and Jackson's striking confidence collapses the way it did against Marcos and Figueiredo, Barcelos can steal rounds by staying busier on the feet and landing the cleaner shots in close. Judges can be fooled by forward pressure from a shorter fighter.

**#3 -- Montel Jackson wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Jackson has proven he can find the finish — Rani Yahya and Blackshear both went out in the first round. Barcelos has been knocked out before and is aging into a window where chin damage accumulates faster. If Jackson lands a clean left hand or catches Barcelos coming in early, a stoppage is live. Unlikely given Barcelos's recent defensive approach, but the power differential and the reach advantage create genuine opportunity.

**#4 -- Raoni Barcelos wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos submitted Quinonez and showed a submission attempt against Talbott in his recent run. If the fight hits the mat — which is a real possibility given Jackson's wrestling — and Jackson gives up position trying to improve, Barcelos has the jiu-jitsu background to capitalize. This requires Jackson's wrestling to backfire, which is unlikely given how the grappling styles match up.

- His striking career averages look functional (3.09 SLpM, 52% accuracy), but his last two fights before the Blackshear KO reveal something concerning: against Marcos he threw at 0.27 SLpM with 33% accuracy, and against Figueiredo he managed 0.53 SLpM at 40%. Those aren't volume issues — that's a fighter who stops trusting his hands the moment the opponent is credible.

- The Blackshear KO at 0:18 of Round 1 inflated his last-3 striking average wildly. Strip that out and you're looking at a fighter who is generating almost nothing offensively on the feet against meaningful competition.

- His 61% strike defense and 1.40 absorbed per minute career average look excellent — but that absorption rate is almost certainly a byproduct of controlling range and keeping fights short with quick wrestling. Against Figueiredo, he was there all three rounds and that number looked very different.

- At 34, his reach advantage in this division is exceptional — 75 inches at bantamweight is rare. He knows how to use it to manage distance, jab behind it, and set up level changes. That's the stylistic backbone of his southpaw game.

- He has been knocked out before and has a 196-day layoff coming in. His chin has never been the issue, but the question is whether his striking confidence — already fragile against elite opponents — is further eroded after the Figueiredo loss.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- Barcelos is a high-volume striker by career averages — 4.92 SLpM — but his last three fights tell a completely different story. He averaged 1.07, 0.40, and 0.53 SLpM in those bouts, each a decision win. That gap between career identity and current output is enormous and suggests he has completely reinvented his approach, leaning into position and defensive work rather than offense.

- At 38, he has absorbed 4.40 significant strikes per minute across his UFC career — one of the higher absorption rates you'll see for a fighter still competing at this level. He has been knocked out (Umar Nurmagomedov, Round 1) and has gone the distance losing multiple decisions. The chin has taken meaningful mileage.

- His takedown defense is genuinely elite at 87%, which is the single most important number in this matchup. He cannot be easily taken down — and that is the one thing Jackson does best.

- Despite being labeled as a striker historically, his last three fights show him leaning heavily on takedowns and controlling output: 1.0 TD per 15 minutes across all three, with the Talbott fight showing 3.0 TD attempts per 15 minutes and a submission attempt. He is adapting to survive, not to finish.

- He has won three straight decisions against Ricky Simon, Cody Garbrandt, and Payton Talbott — none of them easy outs. That's real winning form at bantamweight depth, even if the performances were not exciting.

- The 8-inch reach disadvantage against Jackson is severe. At 67 inches, Barcelos will be fighting inside constantly, which plays directly into Jackson's jab-and-level-change game. Getting inside against a 75-inch southpaw with good wrestling is a difficult and punishing assignment.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this fight is that Jackson's primary weapon — the wrestling — runs directly into Barcelos's best defensive skill — an 87% takedown defense rate. Jackson shoots at 63% accuracy and takes down most opponents, but Barcelos is not most opponents on the mat side. If Jackson commits to wrestling and Barcelos is consistently stuffing those attempts, Jackson loses the main mechanism he relies on and is forced to fight a stand-up battle he has shown very little comfort in against quality competition. That is the matchup problem for the favorite.

On the feet, the 8-inch reach gap is decisive in Jackson's favor. Barcelos at 67 inches has to walk through jabs and level changes just to get into his range. Historically, Barcelos was a high-volume pressure fighter who could absorb punishment and keep coming — but the last three fights suggest that version of Barcelos no longer exists. He's fighting conservatively, winning on control and positioning, and generating almost nothing offensively. That's a fighter who is managing a declining toolkit, not improving one. At 38, with meaningful knockout damage in his history, the aggressive forward-pressure version of Barcelos that his career averages imply is not who Jackson is actually facing.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Jackson — he is four years younger, and the age curve matters in a sport where absorbing damage compounds over time. Barcelos at 38, carrying significant career mileage and a knockout loss on his record, is past the point where his body will respond the way it once did. Jackson's 196-day layoff is a mild concern but not disqualifying for a 3-round undercard fight. The flip side: if Barcelos's elite takedown defense nullifies the wrestling and Jackson reverts to the passive, low-output striking he showed against Marcos and Figueiredo, this becomes a very competitive fight that Barcelos — a veteran who knows how to steal rounds — is capable of winning.

**Flip conditions worth noting:** If Jackson's wrestling lands consistently in rounds two and three, this is a comfortable decision win for him. If Barcelos stuffs the first two or three shots and Jackson mentally abandons the takedown game, Barcelos's path to a decision widens considerably. The reach advantage keeps Jackson safe on the feet, but safe and winning are different things.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Montel Jackson wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Jackson's reach advantage, southpaw angles, and wrestling threat allow him to control range and dictate terms across three rounds without needing a finish. Even if his takedown success is limited by Barcelos's defense, the threat of the shot keeps Barcelos from fully committing offensively, and Jackson can win on distance management and activity without getting hurt. Barcelos's recent output has been too low to out-work him.

**#2 -- Raoni Barcelos wins by split or unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Barcelos has beaten three legitimate opponents in a row winning rounds through positioning and small-margins work. If he successfully defends two or three early takedown attempts and Jackson's striking confidence collapses the way it did against Marcos and Figueiredo, Barcelos can steal rounds by staying busier on the feet and landing the cleaner shots in close. Judges can be fooled by forward pressure from a shorter fighter.

**#3 -- Montel Jackson wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Jackson has proven he can find the finish — Rani Yahya and Blackshear both went out in the first round. Barcelos has been knocked out before and is aging into a window where chin damage accumulates faster. If Jackson lands a clean left hand or catches Barcelos coming in early, a stoppage is live. Unlikely given Barcelos's recent defensive approach, but the power differential and the reach advantage create genuine opportunity.

**#4 -- Raoni Barcelos wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos submitted Quinonez and showed a submission attempt against Talbott in his recent run. If the fight hits the mat — which is a real possibility given Jackson's wrestling — and Jackson gives up position trying to improve, Barcelos has the jiu-jitsu background to capitalize. This requires Jackson's wrestling to backfire, which is unlikely given how the grappling styles match up.
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->

Confidence: 54%

The line has moved from Barcelos opening as a -185 favorite to Jackson now sitting at -190, which means the market has shifted meaningfully off Barcelos and toward Jackson. That's a significant reprice, and it's worth understanding why before fading it. Jackson brings the youth advantage (34 vs. 38), the enormous reach edge, the southpaw stance, and the wrestling threat. Those are real factors and the market appears to be pricing them correctly.

The case for a lean on Barcelos rests on two things: the 87% takedown defense that neutralizes Jackson's best weapon, and the pattern in Jackson's recent fights showing he generates almost nothing offensively when he can't get the fight to the mat. If Barcelos stays upright and defends takedowns, Jackson becomes a low-output fighter trying to win on reach management alone — which is winnable, but far from certain.

Line: -190

De-vigged true probability: approximately 62.3%
Assessed probability: 54% (Jackson), 46% (Barcelos)

Assessed probability: 46%

There is a meaningful gap between the market's implied probability on Barcelos and where the analysis lands. The data on both fighters' intangibles is unavailable from Tapology, which limits certainty on camp quality, weight-cut history, and short-notice status.

**Target line:** The bet loses its value if Barcelos shortens past +140. At +186 or longer, there is enough padding to justify the lean.

## Marcus Buchecha -- Style & Profile

- The greatest Brazilian jiu-jitsu competitor of all time, but his UFC career tells a different story than his grappling legend suggests. In eight UFC fights, he has zero submission attempts per 15 minutes and a 16% takedown accuracy — the wrestling that was supposed to be his path to dominance simply hasn't worked at heavyweight.
- His striking is minimal by design. He lands just 1.70 significant strikes per minute, and in his last UFC fight against Kennedy Nzechukwu, that dropped to a barely visible 0.13 per minute — he essentially threw nothing for 15 minutes and walked away with a draw.
- The Buday loss was instructive: he managed zero takedowns in a decision loss, which means when the grappling isn't working, there is no Plan B. He has no meaningful striking volume to fall back on and no submission attempts to threaten from the guard.
- At 36, he is past the age cliff for men in the lower weight classes, and there is no evidence of chin durability or cardio resilience that would offset that. His last two fights have produced a loss and a draw with almost no offensive output in either.
- Moving from light heavyweight competition to heavyweight and fighting at 255 lbs, he has struggled to impose his physical advantages on opponents who match his size. His 77-inch reach is actually shorter than Spann's 79 inches, removing any positional edge at range.
- The line shortening from -110 to -150 on Buchecha suggests sharp money or informed movement in his direction, but the actual fight data does not yet support a fighter who can finish or control opponents at this level.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Ryan Spann -- Style & Profile

- Spann is a chaos agent — his last ten UFC fights include five finishes in Round 1, zero decision wins, and knockout losses sprinkled in between. He either ends the fight fast or gets ended. There is almost nothing in between.
- His submission game is his sharpest tool. Against Ovince Saint Preux he finished in 1:35; against Ion Cutelaba in 2:22; against Lukasz Brzeski in 2:47 — all Round 1 submissions. When he gets on top or takes the back, opponents don't survive long. His 1.8 submission attempts per 15 minutes career average undersells how decisive those attempts tend to be.
- The striking numbers are ugly: 39% accuracy, 43% strike defense, absorbing 4.14 significant strikes per minute over his career. He has been knocked out by Bogdan Guskov and Waldo Cortes Acosta, both in Round 2, and the Cortes Acosta KO came from a fighter known primarily as a brawler — that's a chin concern.
- He's been knocked out twice in his last four fights. Both stoppages came in the second round, which suggests he can take early fire but breaks down when the fight extends and he takes accumulating damage. This is not a durable chin — it's one that holds up for a round and then cracks.
- The most recent fight, the Brzeski win, was encouraging from a grappling standpoint: 5.39 takedowns per 15 minutes and nearly 11 submission attempts per 15 minutes in that fight alone. The grappling was sharp. The question is whether he can get to that grappling against a jiu-jitsu black belt who will be comfortable on the mat.
- At 34 with a 280-day layoff, the ring rust question is real. He hasn't fought since July 2025, and his last few camps have produced inconsistent results — dominant grappling one fight, getting knocked cold the next.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup is defined by a strange irony: the man with the greatest grappling résumé in the sport can't seem to get fights to the ground in the UFC, while his opponent — a career light heavyweight who finishes most of his wins by submission — is actually the more active and dangerous grappler based on recent UFC data. Spann's 34% takedown accuracy and 1.8 submission attempts per 15 minutes aren't elite numbers, but they're real. Buchecha's 16% takedown accuracy and zero submission attempts in UFC competition are not numbers that inspire confidence, regardless of what he accomplished in gi competition.

The striking matchup is a mess for both fighters, but in different ways. Buchecha barely throws — his 1.70 strikes per minute career average and his near-total offensive shutdown in the Nzechukwu draw paint a picture of a fighter who either can't find his range or is too hesitant to engage. Spann throws more but absorbs a tremendous amount (4.14 per minute career), and his 43% strike defense is poor. If Buchecha can find any rhythm striking — and his 49% accuracy suggests he connects when he does throw — he can potentially land something meaningful against Spann's leaky defense. But Spann's path is simpler: drag Buchecha into a scramble, threaten the submission, or land the right shot on a fighter who is 36 and hasn't shown he can handle sustained offense.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Spann on paper despite his longer layoff. Buchecha's age (36), the total offensive futility of his last two fights, and the fact that he simply has not shown he can impose his jiu-jitsu on UFC-level opponents are all working against him. Spann's 280-day layoff is a genuine concern, and his chin has shown it doesn't hold up to volume in Rounds 2 and 3 — but in a 3-round fight where he's looking for a first-round finish, that matters less. Both fighters would love to end this early. The question is which man's finishing game actually functions at this level.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 -- Ryan Spann wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: Spann has finished his last three submission wins in the first round, all within three minutes. Buchecha's UFC grappling record is paradoxically weak — zero submission attempts and poor takedown accuracy — meaning Spann may actually be the more dangerous man in scrambles. If Spann gets on top or takes the back early, Buchecha's jiu-jitsu credentials don't automatically translate to UFC submission defense.

**#2 -- Marcus Buchecha wins by decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Buchecha can keep the fight standing and use his size advantage to smother Spann's offense, this grinds to a decision the same way his recent fights have. Spann has never won by decision in his UFC career, and if his finishing attempts are neutralized, he has no fallback. Buchecha's 49% striking accuracy is good enough to win ugly if he's willing to engage more than he did against Nzechukwu.

**#3 -- Ryan Spann wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Spann knocked out Dominick Reyes and Misha Cirkunov in the first round, both credible light heavyweights. Buchecha's chin is untested at a high level, and at 36, he's at the age where accumulated damage begins to compound. If Spann lands flush early, there is real stoppage potential given how little punishment Buchecha has appeared to take in his UFC career — sometimes an untested chin is a cracked chin waiting to be found.

**#4 -- Marcus Buchecha wins by TKO/ground-and-pound, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Buchecha manages to get Spann down and work from top position — which he has rarely done but remains theoretically his strength — his ground-and-pound could produce a stoppage. Spann's chin erodes under volume, and if Buchecha's size advantage finally asserts itself on the mat late in the fight, there is a stoppage path here.

## Rodolfo Vieira -- Style & Profile

- Vieira built his UFC career almost entirely on submission grappling — five of his six wins have come by sub, and his ground game (particularly back takes and rear-naked chokes) is genuinely elite. His striking has always been a vehicle to close distance, not a weapon in its own right.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jackson McVey -- Style & Profile

- McVey is a volume striker by output — he's landing nearly 6 significant strikes per minute in his UFC career, an elite rate for the division. The problem is that his 23% strike defense is essentially no defense at all, among the worst numbers you'll see at middleweight.
- His accuracy swings wildly between fights: 79% against Reese, 27% against Ferreira. That inconsistency suggests his output depends heavily on whether the opponent gives him space and angles, not on a repeatable, technical system.
- Both UFC losses came by submission — once in Round 1, once in Round 2. He's shown no grappling activity whatsoever (0 takedown attempts, 0 submission attempts), and his 0% takedown defense makes him completely unequipped for the ground game. When fights leave the feet, McVey is in serious trouble.
- As a southpaw standing 6'4", he has obvious physical tools to work from the outside. But his strike absorption rate of 3.82 per minute is high for someone who wins by punching fast — it suggests he's trading rather than controlling range, which becomes a liability against anyone who can close the distance and clinch.
- His layoff of 168 days is within normal range, and at 27 he's on the right side of the age curve. The concern isn't rustiness — it's whether two consecutive submission losses have exposed a structural hole in his game that he hasn't addressed.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Sedriques Dumas -- Style & Profile

- Dumas is a low-volume, grind-and-decide fighter when everything goes right — three of his four UFC wins came by unanimous decision, and he's not putting people away. His 2.0 significant strikes per minute is well below average, and his 44% accuracy tells you this is a methodical, not explosive, striker.
- His recent form is alarming. He has been knocked out in Round 1 twice in the last two years — by Nursulton Ruziboev and Michal Oleksiejczuk — and then submitted in Round 2 by Donte Johnson. That's three finishes in four fights, two of them early KOs against fighters with real power. The chin has been tested and failed repeatedly.
- Against Oleksiejczuk, he landed only 11.5% of his strikes and was outputting just over 1 strike per minute before getting stopped. Against Donte Johnson, he managed 0.47 strikes per minute before the submission. His offensive production under pressure collapses.
- His takedown defense is 33%, which is genuinely poor. He attempts takedowns himself at a modest rate (1.34 per 15 minutes, 31% accuracy), but those numbers are dragged up by older fights — in his last three, he shot zero takedowns against Oleksiejczuk and Johnson, converting a couple against Reese in a no-contest that lasted under a minute.
- At 30, Dumas isn't old, but he has accumulated a lot of damage in a short window. The pattern of early KO losses combined with deteriorating offensive output paints a picture of a fighter whose best version — the gritty decision-grinder — may have passed.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important question in this matchup is whether McVey can keep it standing, and the answer is probably yes — but that comes with a major caveat. McVey throws nearly three times as many significant strikes per minute as Dumas (5.87 vs. 2.00), and Dumas' 55% strike defense sounds reasonable until you remember that 55% means he's getting touched nearly half the time by an opponent throwing constantly. Against McVey's volume, that number becomes punishing. The southpaw angle is an additional problem for Dumas, who has faced orthodox fighters in the majority of his recent bouts. McVey's left hand from that angle is a genuine threat.

The other side of the coin is grim for McVey. Dumas, even in diminished form, has a wrestling background and 33% takedown defense conceded means little when his opponent has never once attempted a takedown in his UFC career and defends them at 0%. If Dumas can get McVey against the cage and drag him down, this fight looks completely different. McVey's two UFC losses both ended on the ground in Round 2 and Round 1 respectively — he has no answer for grappling. The tension in this fight is a striker who can't defend takedowns against a grappler who has looked completely lost on the feet lately. Dumas' path runs through the clinch and the takedown. McVey's path runs through keeping it at range and landing first and often.

Given Dumas' recent chin issues — two R1 KO losses by fighters with real power — absorbing McVey's volume early is a serious concern. McVey isn't a traditional power finisher, but the combination of southpaw angles, high output, and Dumas' documented vulnerability to early pressure creates a real finish threat. The net intangible picture is roughly neutral: both fighters are coming off losses, similar layoffs, similar ages. McVey's structural edge on the feet is real but fragile given his complete defensive liability once grabbed.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- McVey wins by TKO/KO, Rounds 1-2**
Likelihood: High
Why: McVey's volume at nearly 6 strikes per minute against a fighter who has been stopped twice by early pressure in his last two years is the most likely scenario. Dumas' output has collapsed under pressure in recent fights, and with 55% strike defense against a high-volume southpaw, he's going to absorb significant punishment from the opening bell. McVey doesn't need one-punch power if Dumas is eating 6 strikes a minute — the accumulation itself creates a stoppage opportunity.

**#2 -- Dumas wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: McVey's 0% takedown defense is the most exploitable number in this entire dataset, and if Dumas reverts to the grappling identity that produced his UFC wins, he has a clear path. McVey has been submitted in both his UFC losses without mounting any ground resistance. The question is whether Dumas can weather McVey's early output long enough to get the fight down — if he survives the first flurry and clinches, history says McVey has no answer.

**#3 -- McVey wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Dumas' recent chin concerns are slightly overstated and he's able to survive rounds by keeping distance, McVey's volume should still win him the scorecards cleanly. At nearly three times Dumas' output and with the southpaw advantage, McVey would dominate the stats across three rounds. This is the backup path if the finish doesn't come.

**#4 -- Dumas wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This requires Dumas to successfully neutralize McVey on the feet while accumulating enough control time to win rounds — a version of Dumas we haven't seen recently. It's not impossible if McVey's defensive liabilities (23% strike defense) lead to a more measured exchange than his output numbers suggest, but Dumas hasn't won a fight convincingly since 2023 and his offensive production has been near-zero in recent outings.

The tension in this fight is a striker who can't defend takedowns against a grappler who has looked completely lost on the feet lately. Dumas' path runs through the clinch and the takedown. McVey's path runs through keeping it at range and landing first and often.

Given Dumas' recent chin issues — two R1 KO losses by fighters with real power — absorbing McVey's volume early is a serious concern. McVey isn't a traditional power finisher, but the combination of southpaw angles, high output, and Dumas' documented vulnerability to early pressure creates a real finish threat. The net intangible picture is roughly neutral: both fighters are coming off losses, similar layoffs, similar ages. McVey's structural edge on the feet is real but fragile given his complete defensive liability once grabbed.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- McVey wins by TKO/KO, Rounds 1-2**
Likelihood: High
Why: McVey's volume at nearly 6 strikes per minute against a fighter who has been stopped twice by early pressure in his last two years is the most likely scenario. Dumas' output has collapsed under pressure in recent fights, and with 55% strike defense against a high-volume southpaw, he's going to absorb significant punishment from the opening bell. McVey doesn't need one-punch power if Dumas is eating 6 strikes a minute — the accumulation itself creates a stoppage opportunity.

**#2 -- Dumas wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: McVey's 0% takedown defense is the most exploitable number in this entire dataset, and if Dumas reverts to the grappling identity that produced his UFC wins, he has a clear path. McVey has been submitted in both his UFC losses without mounting any ground resistance. The question is whether Dumas can weather McVey's early output long enough to get the fight down — if he survives the first flurry and clinches, history says McVey has no answer.

**#3 -- McVey wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Dumas' recent chin concerns are slightly overstated and he's able to survive rounds by keeping distance, McVey's volume should still win him the scorecards cleanly. At nearly three times Dumas' output and with the southpaw advantage, McVey would dominate the stats across three rounds. This is the backup path if the finish doesn't come.

**#4 -- Dumas wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This requires Dumas to successfully neutralize McVey on the feet while accumulating enough control time to win rounds — a version of Dumas we haven't seen recently. It's not impossible if McVey's defensive liabilities (23% strike defense) lead to a more measured exchange than his output numbers suggest, but Dumas hasn't won a fight convincingly since 2023 and his offensive production has been near-zero in recent outings.
<!--END-->

Confidence: 63%

McVey opened as a slight favorite and has since moved to -190, meaning the market has moved firmly in his direction as more information has come in. This is not reverse line movement — the line has tracked straight toward McVey, suggesting consistent sharp money on his side. The movement is significant: Dumas opened as a -180 favorite and now sits as a +130 dog, a dramatic swing that reflects real information. The market agrees with the structural read here.

The case for McVey is straightforward: he is a high-volume striker with a meaningful southpaw advantage facing a fighter who has been finished early three times in his last four completed bouts. Dumas' strike defense and recent form under offensive pressure are genuinely poor. McVey's volume (nearly 6 strikes per minute) against Dumas' documented chin fragility is a credible finish pathway.

The risk — and it's real — is McVey's 0% takedown defense. Both of his UFC losses ended on the ground, quickly. If Dumas lands a takedown and pins McVey down, that fight changes completely. The bet lives and dies on McVey keeping it standing long enough to do damage.

**The math:**
- Current line: McVey -190
- Implied probability: 65.5%
- De-vigged true probability: 59.4%
- Assessed probability: 63%
- Stake: 1.2 units (quarter Kelly)

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB**

McVey has been submitted twice and Dumas has been knocked out twice in recent fights — this is not a fight that is likely to see three full rounds. If you can get that prop at -130 or better, the matchup structure supports it. De-vig before taking it and require at least 5 percentage points of edge over the market's implied number.

**Flip conditions:** If McVey's 0% takedown defense reflects a specific defensive system he's working on in camp (unknown from available data), the edge narrows. If Dumas' recent KO losses were against opponents with elite power not comparable to McVey, discount the chin-fragility concern. If McVey's camp situation or weight cut has any red flags (data unavailable here), move this to a lean or pass.

## Assuerio Silva -- Style & Profile

- This fight is being listed with a 6,944-day layoff — that's roughly 19 years since Silva last competed. His UFC career ended in 2007, and there is no meaningful recent data to work with. Every stat in his profile is nearly two decades old and should be treated as historical record, not predictive information.
- In his UFC tenure, Silva was primarily a grappler who could mix in striking — he averaged 2.66 takedowns per 15 minutes and 1.8 submission attempts per 15 minutes. However, his takedown accuracy was only 34%, and his submissions came more from catching opponents than from a high-volume submission game.
- His striking was minimal. He landed just 1.42 significant strikes per minute while absorbing 4.20 — meaning he was consistently out-struck. His strike defense sat at 54%, not good enough to survive against anyone with real punching volume.
- His last three UFC fights were all losses: decision defeats to Cheick Kongo and Tim Sylvia, and a first-round submission loss to Brandon Vera. He was knocked out by Aleksander Emelianenko in that same stretch. The fighter described by these numbers was already being overwhelmed at the UFC level in 2006–2007.
- At 51 years old with a two-decade absence, the physical deterioration is not a concern — it is a certainty. Chin durability, cardio, reflexes, and timing all decline sharply after age 35 for heavyweight-adjacent fighters. Whatever athletic baseline he had in 2007 no longer exists.
- The only things his record confirms are two early finishes over regional-level opponents (Yamamoto in 11 seconds, Overeem by submission) — wins that were nearly 20 years ago and carry no predictive weight whatsoever.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Michelle Montague -- Style & Profile

- Montague enters undefeated at 7-0, but her UFC sample is extremely thin — just one UFC fight on record, a unanimous decision win over Luana Carolina in September 2025. Almost everything meaningful about her current form has to be inferred from her pre-UFC career, which substantially limits analytical confidence.
- Her UFC career numbers show a high takedown rate (5.00 per 15 minutes) at 71% accuracy — that is genuinely elite efficiency, even if built on a single-fight sample. She gets the fight where she wants it on the ground and doesn't miss often when she shoots.
- Her striking in that lone UFC outing was cautious — 0.67 significant strikes per minute at 31% accuracy. She was not trying to win a standup fight. Whether that conservatism reflects her full skill set or was a game-plan-specific choice against Carolina is unclear with one data point.
- Her strike defense of 41% is a genuine concern. She absorbed 3.20 significant strikes per minute in her UFC career average, and fighters who absorb at that rate while posting sub-45% defense tend to get hurt when they step up in competition. For now, the one-fight sample makes it hard to know whether this is a real vulnerability or statistical noise.
- As a southpaw, she carries a natural stance advantage against orthodox opponents, particularly those who haven't seen southpaw pressure recently — though in this matchup, that detail is almost irrelevant given the state of her opponent.
- She is fighting at 135 pounds. Silva is listed at 241 pounds. This matchup as presented involves a catastrophic weight discrepancy — more than 100 pounds — which raises serious questions about the legitimacy of this pairing as a competitive contest.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

There is a fundamental problem with analyzing this matchup in conventional terms: the two fighters do not appear to be competing in the same weight class. Montague is listed at 135 pounds, Silva at 241 pounds. A 106-pound weight differential at the professional level is not a stylistic disadvantage — it is a physical impossibility for any real competition to take place on equal footing. If this fight is occurring as listed, the structural mismatch transcends any tactical analysis. No amount of grappling efficiency, striking accuracy, or cardio advantage closes that gap.

Setting the weight issue aside and treating this purely as a biographical matchup: Silva's entire competitive history is from 2007 and earlier. His stats — the strikes absorbed, the takedowns attempted, the submission losses — belong to a different era of MMA and a version of this athlete that has not existed for nearly two decades. Montague, though lightly tested at the UFC level, is an active competitor who fought in September 2025. She is current; he is not. Her grappling, which is the most reliable tool in her kit based on available numbers, would theoretically give her a path to controlling and finishing a much larger but completely ring-rusted opponent — but again, none of the normal analytical frameworks apply when one fighter outweighs the other by over 100 pounds.

The net intangible picture is overwhelming in one direction. Silva carries a 19-year layoff, an age of 51, and a competitive record that ended on a four-fight losing streak. Montague is 32, recently active, and undefeated. If this fight happens as described, there is no version of this analysis where Silva represents a credible threat or where the outcome is genuinely uncertain from an analytical standpoint.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Montague wins by TKO/KO or Submission, Rounds 1–2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Silva has not competed in nearly two decades, is 51 years old, and was already being finished and out-struck at the UFC level in 2006–2007. A currently active fighter with elite takedown efficiency and 19 years of physical advantage should be able to impose her will almost immediately. Whether the finish comes standing or on the ground, the expectation is a stoppage well before the final bell.

**#2 -- Montague wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Silva's sheer size (if this fight is taking place at catchweight or under unusual circumstances) could make the finish more difficult than expected, and Montague's striking volume in her lone UFC fight was low. A scenario where she controls but doesn't finish is possible if she fights cautiously, though this remains the less likely outcome given the overall mismatch.

**#3 -- Silva wins by any method**
Likelihood: Very Low
Why: Mathematically, early KO threats exist for any fighter who can land a punch. Silva has a massive weight advantage on paper. But the combination of his age, two-decade layoff, ring rust, and the fact that he was already losing to top-tier competition in 2007 makes this scenario almost purely theoretical. It cannot be ruled out entirely — early shots land sometimes — but it is not a realistic betting scenario.
