analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jafel Filho -- Style & Profile

- Filho is fundamentally a submission grappler who moves forward and drags fights to the mat. He averages 2.52 takedowns per 15 minutes across his UFC career with a 45% accuracy rate, and his submission attempt rate of 2.2 per 15 minutes reflects someone who is actively hunting the finish once he gets there — not just controlling.
- His last three fights reveal a stark split between what he does well and what he doesn't. When he gets the fight to the mat, he finishes (two R1 sub wins over Osbourne and Carpenter). When the fight stays standing, he struggles — his loss to Allan Nascimento was a unanimous decision where he was out-struck on the feet while attempting to close distance.
- His striking output is low and deliberate: 1.85 significant strikes landed per minute career, with a career accuracy of 41%. He's not throwing volume — he's touching and closing, using striking as a setup for the clinch and takedown rather than as a standalone weapon.
- He absorbed just 1.62 strikes per minute career, one of the lower absorption rates in the flyweight division. That reflects a fighter who does not stand and trade — he pressures forward, clinches, or exits. He's never been knocked out in his UFC career, and his only losses are a submission (to Mokaev, one of the best grapplers in the division) and a decision (to Nascimento on the feet).
- At 32, Filho is in his prime and coming off a first-round submission win. His 196-day layoff is standard camp length — nothing concerning there.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Cody Durden -- Style & Profile

- Durden's career numbers paint the picture of a high-activity fighter who takes heavy punishment for it: 3.34 significant strikes landed per minute, but 4.19 absorbed per minute. For every strike he lands, he absorbs 1.25 in return. That exchange rate is losing math over time, and it's catching up to him.
- His last three fights are a four-alarm fire. He was knocked out in eleven seconds by Jose Ochoa (a clean R1 KO with a non-elite striker), submitted in R2 by Nascimento, and then decision-lost to Tumendemberel while throwing only 0.27 significant strikes per minute — a number so low it suggests either a physical breakdown or a camp that completely misread the gameplan. He has lost five of his last six fights.
- He has been knocked out twice in his last four UFC appearances (by Bruno Silva in 2023 and by Ochoa in 2025). The Ochoa loss is particularly telling — Ochoa is not a power striker by reputation, and Durden folded in eleven seconds. That R2 KO pattern has shown up multiple times, and the chin is no longer reliable.
- Durden is a southpaw with decent takedown numbers (3.89 per 15 minutes, 46% accuracy), but his grappling attack in recent fights has become sporadic. His submission rate is just 0.6 attempts per 15 minutes, meaning when he does get takedowns, he's largely looking to grind rather than finish — and his takedown defense sits at 60%, leaving him vulnerable to shots from opponents with real wrestling.
- At 35, with a 17-10-1 record and back-to-back-to-back losses, Durden is in a phase where the physical tools are eroding faster than the record can disguise. His short 49-day turnaround from his last loss is also a yellow flag — whether that reflects confidence or desperation, it's a rapid return for a fighter absorbing the volume he has been.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central question in this fight is whether Durden can do anything meaningful on the feet before Filho gets a clinch or a takedown, and the honest answer based on recent evidence is: almost certainly not. Filho is a forward-pressure grappler whose entire game is built around closing distance and imposing control. Durden's 60% takedown defense means he stops roughly four out of ten takedown attempts from fighters at this level — against someone averaging 2.52 per 15 minutes with specific finishing ability off the mat, that number is a serious liability. Filho's path is straightforward: walk Durden to the fence, clinch, take him down, and work submissions. He has finished four of his last five wins by submission, all in the first round or early in the fight.

Durden's theoretical path runs through striking — he does land at 3.34 significant strikes per minute, and as a southpaw he brings a wrinkle Filho needs to respect. But the numbers from Durden's last three fights are so alarming that it's hard to weigh his career striking averages as meaningful. In those three fights combined, he threw at 0.27, 1.74, and 4.38 strikes per minute — wildly inconsistent output that suggests a fighter who no longer has a reliable fight template. More concerning, he has now been finished multiple times against opponents who were not elite finishers. His chin has been tested and failed recently, and Filho — while not a power striker — is an active submission threat who will keep Durden uncomfortable and constantly under physical pressure.

The net intangible picture also favors Filho clearly. Filho is three years younger, coming off a clean finish win, and on a layoff that represents normal camp recovery. Durden is 35, on a five-fight losing streak, coming back in 49 days, and carrying documented stopping losses from both KO and submission. There is no angle from which Durden's situation looks like one that produces an upset here.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Jafel Filho wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: Filho has submitted four of his last five opponents, all inside the first round except his loss. Durden's 60% takedown defense will not stop Filho from getting the fight down, and once Filho has top position, his 2.2 submission attempts per 15 minutes reflects active hunting. Durden has been submitted twice in his last four fights, including a second-round sub loss to the same opponent Filho has beaten. The finishing blueprint is clear.

**#2 -- Jafel Filho wins by submission, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Durden has historically been slippery in scrambles and can extend fights by staying active even when losing. If he survives R1 through movement or a strong defensive sequence, Filho will return to the same gameplan in R2 — and the cumulative pressure of a grappler of this quality will eventually find its way to the back or a limb. Filho has shown he doesn't gas out; his absorption numbers are low precisely because he doesn't stand and brawl late.

**#3 -- Jafel Filho wins by TKO/ground-and-pound, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Filho is not a heavy striker, but Durden's chin has shown real fragility and he can be stopped when hurt on the ground. If Filho takes his back or achieves a dominant position and lands hammerfists, referee intervention is a realistic alternative finish — particularly given that R1 and R2 are exactly when Durden's losses have been coming.

**#4 -- Cody Durden wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Durden's only realistic path is to keep the fight standing, use his southpaw jab to manage distance, and survive three rounds. His 44% striking accuracy and forward pressure in his better performances show he can be functional on the feet. But this requires Filho's takedowns to fail at a rate well above what the numbers suggest, and it requires Durden to perform well beyond what his last three fights indicate he is currently capable of.
analysis_sections.betting:
**STRONG PLAY — Jafel Filho**

Confidence: 87%

The case here is not complicated, and the math confirms what the eye test shows. Filho at -715 converts to approximately 87.7% implied probability. De-vigged against Durden's +330 (23.3% implied, 108.5% combined before de-vig — actually 81.9% for Filho, 18.1% for Durden after de-vig), Filho's true market probability sits around 82%. My assessed probability for Filho winning this fight is approximately 87%, reflecting a clear and well-documented structural mismatch.

That said, -715 is a number that requires scrutiny. To be useful as a bet, the edge has to survive the juice. At -715, you are risking $715 to win $100. The implied probability of 87.7% is close to my assessed probability of 87%, which means the edge at the current price is thin — roughly in the noise. Betting -715 on a fight where you assess 87% probability is not materially profitable over time because you're basically getting paid at market price with no surplus.

**Line:** Jafel Filho -715 (current)
**Implied probability:** 87.7%
**De-vigged true probability:** ~82%
**Assessed probability:** ~87%
**Edge at -715:** Marginal — approximately 5 percentage points over the de-vigged number, but the juice eats most of it at this price.
**Target line for value:** -500 or shorter. At -500 (83.3% implied, ~80% de-vigged), the edge over the true number is cleaner and the math justifies action.
**Stake at -715:** The quarter-Kelly sizing at this price and edge level produces a very small unit recommendation — approximately 0.5 units if you have access to -500 or better, and effectively zero at -715.

**Practical recommendation:** If you are seeing Filho at prices shorter than -500 anywhere (DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM), the play is Filho for 0.5 units. At -715, the pick is correct but the price has no remaining value — pass the moneyline at that number.

**Both fighters:** Filho has five or more UFC fights with recent activity — solid data. Durden has extensive UFC history, also solid data. No low-tier data confidence caps apply.

**Market read:** Filho opened -400 and has moved sharply to -715. Durden opened around -549 (in error in some market contexts — this reflects the original "reverse" pricing) and is now +330. The movement has been in one direction — massive sharp and public agreement on Filho. This is not reverse line movement; the line has moved in alignment with the obvious structural read. The market has already done the work here, and the price has been so fully extracted that the value proposition has narrowed to almost nothing on the moneyline.

**Hedge verdict — NO ARB.** Filho -715 (87.7% implied) + Durden +330 (23.3% implied) = 111% combined. The juice is steep on both sides. No arb exists. Take the value pick on the best available moneyline (Filho at the shortest price you can find across books), do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** The most actionable bet in this fight is **"Fight does not go to decision" / "Fight ends inside the distance."** Filho has finished four of his last five opponents, Durden has been finished twice in his last four, and there is no scenario where Durden takes Filho down and grinds out a grinding decision win. <!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Jafel Filho -- Style & Profile

- Filho is fundamentally a submission grappler who moves forward and drags fights to the mat. He averages 2.52 takedowns per 15 minutes across his UFC career with a 45% accuracy rate, and his submission attempt rate of 2.2 per 15 minutes reflects someone who is actively hunting the finish once he gets there — not just controlling.
- His last three fights reveal a stark split between what he does well and what he doesn't. When he gets the fight to the mat, he finishes (two R1 sub wins over Osbourne and Carpenter). When the fight stays standing, he struggles — his loss to Allan Nascimento was a unanimous decision where he was out-struck on the feet while attempting to close distance.
- His striking output is low and deliberate: 1.85 significant strikes landed per minute career, with a career accuracy of 41%. He's not throwing volume — he's touching and closing, using striking as a setup for the clinch and takedown rather than as a standalone weapon.
- He absorbed just 1.62 strikes per minute career, one of the lower absorption rates in the flyweight division. That reflects a fighter who does not stand and trade — he pressures forward, clinches, or exits. He's never been knocked out in his UFC career, and his only losses are a submission (to Mokaev, one of the best grapplers in the division) and a decision (to Nascimento on the feet).
- At 32, Filho is in his prime and coming off a first-round submission win. His 196-day layoff is standard camp length — nothing concerning there.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Cody Durden -- Style & Profile

- Durden's career numbers paint the picture of a high-activity fighter who takes heavy punishment for it: 3.34 significant strikes landed per minute, but 4.19 absorbed per minute. For every strike he lands, he absorbs 1.25 in return. That exchange rate is losing math over time, and it's catching up to him.
- His last three fights are a four-alarm fire. He was knocked out in eleven seconds by Jose Ochoa (a clean R1 KO with a non-elite striker), submitted in R2 by Nascimento, and then decision-lost to Tumendemberel while throwing only 0.27 significant strikes per minute — a number so low it suggests either a physical breakdown or a camp that completely misread the gameplan. He has lost five of his last six fights.
- He has been knocked out twice in his last four UFC appearances (by Bruno Silva in 2023 and by Ochoa in 2025). The Ochoa loss is particularly telling — Ochoa is not a power striker by reputation, and Durden folded in eleven seconds. That R2 KO pattern has shown up multiple times, and the chin is no longer reliable.
- Durden is a southpaw with decent takedown numbers (3.89 per 15 minutes, 46% accuracy), but his grappling attack in recent fights has become sporadic. His submission rate is just 0.6 attempts per 15 minutes, meaning when he does get takedowns, he's largely looking to grind rather than finish — and his takedown defense sits at 60%, leaving him vulnerable to shots from opponents with real wrestling.
- At 35, with a 17-10-1 record and back-to-back-to-back losses, Durden is in a phase where the physical tools are eroding faster than the record can disguise. His short 49-day turnaround from his last loss is also a yellow flag — whether that reflects confidence or desperation, it's a rapid return for a fighter absorbing the volume he has been.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central question in this fight is whether Durden can do anything meaningful on the feet before Filho gets a clinch or a takedown, and the honest answer based on recent evidence is: almost certainly not. Filho is a forward-pressure grappler whose entire game is built around closing distance and imposing control. Durden's 60% takedown defense means he stops roughly four out of ten takedown attempts from fighters at this level — against someone averaging 2.52 per 15 minutes with specific finishing ability off the mat, that number is a serious liability. Filho's path is straightforward: walk Durden to the fence, clinch, take him down, and work submissions. He has finished four of his last five wins by submission, all in the first round or early in the fight.

Durden's theoretical path runs through striking — he does land at 3.34 significant strikes per minute, and as a southpaw he brings a wrinkle Filho needs to respect. But the numbers from Durden's last three fights are so alarming that it's hard to weigh his career striking averages as meaningful. In those three fights combined, he threw at 0.27, 1.74, and 4.38 strikes per minute — wildly inconsistent output that suggests a fighter who no longer has a reliable fight template. More concerning, he has now been finished multiple times against opponents who were not elite finishers. His chin has been tested and failed recently, and Filho — while not a power striker — is an active submission threat who will keep Durden uncomfortable and constantly under physical pressure.

The net intangible picture also favors Filho clearly. Filho is three years younger, coming off a clean finish win, and on a layoff that represents normal camp recovery. Durden is 35, on a five-fight losing streak, coming back in 49 days, and carrying documented stopping losses from both KO and submission. There is no angle from which Durden's situation looks like one that produces an upset here.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->

**#1 -- Jafel Filho wins by submission, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: Filho has submitted four of his last five opponents, all inside the first round except his loss. Durden's 60% takedown defense will not stop Filho from getting the fight down, and once Filho has top position, his 2.2 submission attempts per 15 minutes reflects active hunting. Durden has been submitted twice in his last four fights, including a second-round sub loss to the same opponent Filho has beaten. The finishing blueprint is clear.

**#2 -- Jafel Filho wins by submission, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Durden has historically been slippery in scrambles and can extend fights by staying active even when losing. If he survives R1 through movement or a strong defensive sequence, Filho will return to the same gameplan in R2 — and the cumulative pressure of a grappler of this quality will eventually find its way to the back or a limb. Filho has shown he doesn't gas out; his absorption numbers are low precisely because he doesn't stand and brawl late.

Likelihood: Low
Why: Filho is not a heavy striker, but Durden's chin has shown real fragility and he can be stopped when hurt on the ground. Likelihood: Low
Why: Durden's only realistic path is to keep the fight standing, use his southpaw jab to manage distance, and survive three rounds. ## Francis Marshall -- Style & Profile

- Marshall is fundamentally a wrestler who has never fully committed to being one. His career takedown average of 2.86 per 15 minutes is solid, but his striking output (3.39 sig. strikes/min at just 33% accuracy) suggests he throws a lot without landing much — he's more comfortable creating the threat of the takedown than actually closing the show on the feet.
- His last three fights tell two different stories. Against Erik Silva, he was a relentless grappler — 6 takedowns per 15 minutes, a submission finish in under three minutes, and 60% striking accuracy. Against Santos and Buzukja, he was a passive, low-volume grinder landing barely above 1 strike per minute. He performs best when he can drag the fight to the mat early.
- His chin is a real concern. He was knocked out by Isaac Dulgarian in round one, and he has been finished on the feet before. He absorbs 4.03 significant strikes per minute against a career defense rate of just 53% — that's below average for the division, meaning he gets hit, and he gets hit often.
- His submission game is his clearest path to a finish. The Silva win showed he can move quickly from a takedown to a submission finish (6 submission attempts per 15 in that fight), and with 0.6 sub attempts per 15 across his career, it's his highest-percentage finishing route when he gets the fight where he wants it.
- At 27 and fresh off a fight just 56 days ago, there are no age or layoff concerns. The real question is whether he'll show up as the active, aggressive grappler from the Silva fight or the passive point-fighter from the Santos loss.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Lucas Brennan -- Style & Profile

- Brennan is making his UFC debut, and there is essentially no statistical record to analyze from his time in the organization. His UFC striking stats, grappling stats, and fight history all read zero across the board — this analysis rests entirely on his 11-2 pre-UFC record and whatever patterns that record reveals.
- At 11-2, he's clearly been successful outside the UFC, but without knowing the quality of opposition or finish methods from that record, the confidence ceiling on any projection is inherently low. Regional records at 11-2 can mean anything from "elite prospect" to "careful matchmaking" — the distinction matters enormously and the data doesn't resolve it.
- He's a southpaw, which is a real tactical variable. Marshall's recent opponents haven't been flagged as southpaws in the available data, and orthodox fighters routinely struggle with the angle and lead-hand position of a left-handed opponent — particularly in the early rounds before adjustments are made.
- His layoff is listed as N/A, which likely means this is his UFC debut without a clear recorded last-fight date in the system. The debut adjustment applies: first UFC fights carry a meaningful performance discount regardless of pre-UFC record, as fighters adapt to a larger cage, different gloves, and a significant jump in opponent quality.
- Without finish methods from his pre-UFC record, it's impossible to confidently call him a striker, wrestler, or submission artist. The southpaw stance and the 11-2 record are the two real data points. Everything else is projection.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central structural question here is straightforward: can Marshall impose his wrestling on a debutant, or will Brennan's southpaw angles and unknown ceiling complicate that plan? Marshall's best path is exactly what he showed against Silva — a fast, aggressive grappling game that takes the fight to the mat before Brennan can establish rhythm on the feet. When Marshall is active early, he's dangerous. When he stalls and tries to grind on the feet, as he did against Santos, he's beatable. The version of Marshall that shows up in the first two minutes will likely determine the entire fight's trajectory.

Brennan's advantages are harder to quantify precisely because of the data gap, but two things stand out. First, the southpaw stance gives him natural positional advantages against an orthodox opponent — his straight left lands where Marshall's guard doesn't naturally cover, and Marshall's 53% strike defense suggests he's already getting clipped regularly against conventional opponents. If Brennan has any meaningful striking background, those angles could be a real problem early. Second, Marshall's chin has already been tested by a round-one knockout loss, and the fact that he absorbs 4.03 significant strikes per minute at below-average defense means Brennan doesn't need elite power to find him — he just needs to be busy and accurate on the feet.

The net intangible picture tips toward Marshall despite the uncertainty around Brennan. Marshall has UFC experience, a recent active showing, and a clear finishing route in his wrestling and submission game. Brennan carries the debut discount — UFC debuts historically skew toward outcomes that punish stylistic naivety, and without camp quality, training partner quality, or finish-method data, projecting Brennan as a significant threat requires filling in too many blanks optimistically. The sharp money moving Marshall's line from -400 to -550 is consistent with the structural read: this is a fight Marshall is expected to control, and the question is how efficiently he does it.

## Max Griffin -- Style & Profile

- A 40-year-old welterweight who has logged 32 professional fights, Griffin is a durable pressure fighter who earns his wins through volume and forward movement — not clean power. His two KOs in his last ten fights came in 2019–2020; he hasn't finished anyone since.
- His last three performances are genuinely alarming from an output standpoint. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Talita Alencar -- Style & Profile

- Alencar is a grappling-first fighter who wins by controlling range, closing distance, and grinding opponents into submission or decision. Her career UFC output of 3.02 takedowns per 15 minutes is the engine of everything she does offensively — the striking is a setup, not a weapon.
- Her striking volume has been historically low — under 3 significant strikes per minute across her UFC career — and her last three fights confirm the pattern. Her peak output was 1.23 per minute against Carnelossi. She does not win shootouts and doesn't try to.
- What she has done is sharpen her accuracy when she does strike. Her last two fights show accuracy rates of 60% and 53%, both well above her 37% career mark, suggesting she's picking spots more carefully rather than opening up. That's a fighter who has simplified their game, not one declining.
- The submission win over Carnelossi in November 2025 was her first UFC finish in that mode, and it came in the third round — meaning she wore the opponent down before capitalizing. That's a patience-based grappler, not a scrambler who hunts submissions from the outset.
- At 35, she is at or just past the age where women in MMA typically begin showing diminishing returns on explosive athleticism. Her layoff of 175 days is manageable, but any ring rust will show first in her takedown shot timing — already her weakest link at 26% accuracy career-wide.
- One flag worth noting: across five career UFC bouts, she has been unable to finish fights standing at all — zero knockouts or TKOs. Against a striker with Polastri's volume, if the takedowns aren't landing, she has no Plan B on the feet.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Julia Polastri -- Style & Profile

- Polastri is one of the highest-volume strikers in the strawweight division — 6.4 significant strikes per minute career-wide in the UFC is elite output for 115 pounds. She pushes pace, walks opponents down, and wants to make every round feel like a sprint.
- Her five-inch reach advantage over Alencar is real and visible in how she fights — she can operate at a range where she's landing and Alencar isn't, and at 63 inches of reach for a 5'2" fighter, that's an unusually long frame for the division.
- The KO of Karolina Kowalkiewicz in October 2025 is the most relevant recent data point. Kowalkiewicz is a seasoned, durable veteran, and Polastri finished her in the third round — which tells you two things: she carries power into late rounds, and she can crack experienced opponents.
- The Godinez loss is important context. Polastri posted only 0.93 strikes per minute in that fight, her lowest output in the UFC by a wide margin. Godinez is a grappler who slowed her down, dragged her into a clinch-heavy, low-output fight, and won a decision. That is essentially the blueprint Alencar will try to use.
- Her takedown defense sits at 60% — below average for the division and a real vulnerability. She has not been taken down frequently because her opponents have been primarily strikers, but against a dedicated wrestler, that number matters.
- Polastri also absorbs a significant volume herself — 5.4 significant strikes per minute against, second-highest of any stat on her sheet. She wins exchanges, but she does not avoid them. That's a fine trade against fellow strikers but becomes less favorable when a wrestler is closing the distance and clinching.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The structural question in this fight is simple: can Alencar close the distance and get Polastri to the canvas before Polastri's volume accumulates enough damage to win on the scorecards? Polastri holds a five-inch reach advantage, lands at one of the highest rates in the division, and has shown she can maintain output through three rounds — including a late finish in her most recent bout. Every second Alencar spends in the pocket trying to set up takedowns is a second where she is getting hit by a longer, faster striker.

But the Godinez blueprint is sitting right there. Godinez took Polastri's space away, forced clinch work, and neutralized her volume entirely — posting a win at 0.93 strikes per minute against a fighter who typically operates above 4.0. Alencar's approach is similar in structure: she is not going to try to out-strike Polastri, she is going to try to make the fight ugly, get to the body, and get takedowns. The problem is that Alencar's takedown accuracy is 26% career-wide, and she has shown the same low-volume pattern in her last three fights. If her shots are sprawled and she is reset to the feet repeatedly, she is in trouble. Polastri's 60% takedown defense is not impenetrable, but Alencar will need multiple attempts per round just to convert one.

The age and reach gap both tilt toward Polastri. Alencar is seven years older, at 35, which in women's MMA is the age where the athletic margin starts showing. The line movement tells the same story — Polastri opened around +210 and has moved to -200, a significant market shift indicating that sharp money has come in strongly on her side. The betting market has essentially re-evaluated this fight entirely from where it opened, which aligns with what the stats support. Polastri's pace, her reach, her recent finish of a credentialed opponent, and the structural difficulty Alencar faces in executing her game plan all point the same direction.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Julia Polastri wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Polastri's volume and reach make her the consistent threat on the feet, and Alencar's takedown accuracy (26% career) limits how much grappling she can realistically convert against a moving target. If the fight stays largely upright — even with Alencar pressing and clinching — Polastri accumulates enough strikes over three rounds to win on all three cards. The Godinez loss shows she can be slowed, but Alencar is not the same type of grappler as Godinez, and Polastri's output tends to find a level in most fights.

**#2 -- Julia Polastri wins by KO/TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: The KO of Kowalkiewicz in R3 shows Polastri has real finishing power, and she tends to land it late when opponents slow down from absorbing her volume. Alencar has zero KO losses in her UFC career, which suggests a reasonable chin, but she also takes 3.36 strikes per minute on average — and against 6.4 per minute of incoming, that accumulation adds up. A late stoppage is a genuine scenario if Alencar's forward-pressure approach leaves her eating straight shots on the way in.

**#3 -- Talita Alencar wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Alencar executes the Godinez blueprint — constant forward pressure, repeated takedown attempts, clinch control — she can drag Polastri into a low-volume grappling match and steal rounds on control time. She's done it before: three decision wins in her last five fights. The path requires her takedowns to connect at a higher rate than her career average and requires Polastri to have a worse night on the feet than she's shown recently.

**#4 -- Talita Alencar wins by submission, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The Carnelossi sub in November 2025 shows the finishing instinct is there, but it came after wearing an opponent down over three rounds. Against Polastri's pace, getting that level of control is a much harder ask. It's not impossible — if Alencar gets a takedown in the third when both fighters are tired, she has the patience and the positional grappling to work for a finish — but it requires a lot of things to go right first.
